khan_exercises.title: Correlative conjunctions
khan_exercises.description: Learn to recognize pairs of correlative conjunctions in sentences.
topic_path: Parts of speech: the preposition and the conjunction

items.question_content:
**Choose the best conjunction for this sentence.**

Both Avery \_\_\_\_\_\_ Chloe are allergic to sunlight.

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Correlative conjunctions, or conjunctions used in pairs, should join sentence parts, sometimes called “matching parts.”

Common correlative conjunctions include

- Either … or
- Neither … nor
- Both … and
- If … then
Since *"both"* is used in the sentence, we should use its partner, **"and"**.

items.question_content:
**Choose the best conjunction for this sentence.**

We’ll find the kangaroos either by the watering hole \_\_\_\_\_\_ under the shade tree.

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Correlative conjunctions, or conjunctions used in pairs, should join sentence parts, sometimes called “matching parts.”

Common correlative conjunctions include

- Either … or
- Neither … no
- Both … and
- If … then
Since *"either"* is used in the sentence, we should use its partner, **"or"**.

items.question_content:
**Choose the best conjunction for this sentence.**

\_\_\_\_\_\_ Samantha nor her mother had been to Iowa City before.

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Correlative conjunctions, or conjunctions used in pairs, should join sentence parts, sometimes called “matching parts.”

Common correlative conjunctions include

- Either … or
- Neither … no
- Both … and
- If … then
Since *"nor"* is used in the sentence, we should use its partner, **"neither"**.

items.question_content:
**Choose the best conjunction for this sentence.**

If I read one page of this book a night, \_\_\_\_\_\_ I will be finished by the end of the year.

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Correlative conjunctions, or conjunctions used in pairs, should join sentence parts, sometimes called “matching parts.”

Common correlative conjunctions include

- Either … or
- Neither … no
- Both … and
- If … then
Since *"if"* is used in the sentence, we should use its partner, **"then"**.

items.question_content:
**Choose the best conjunction for this sentence.**

Either I’m seeing double, \_\_\_\_\_\_ you are holding up 20 fingers.

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Correlative conjunctions, or conjunctions used in pairs, should join sentence parts, sometimes called “matching parts.”

Common correlative conjunctions include

- Either … or
- Neither … nor
- Both … and
- If … then
Since *"either"* is used in the sentence, we should use its partner, **"or"**.

items.question_content:
**Choose the best conjunction for this sentence.**

Just as my sister took Irish dance classes, \_\_\_\_\_\_ will I.

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Correlative conjunctions, or conjunctions used in pairs, should join sentence parts, sometimes called “matching parts.”

There are many correlative conjunctions, but common ones include

- Either … or
- Neither … no
- Both … and
- If … then
Since *"just as"* is used in the sentence, we should use its partner, **"so"**.

items.question_content:
**Choose the best conjunction for this sentence.**

Mrs. Emmer, a chef, not only dices onions with ease \_\_\_\_\_\_ specializes in lemon meringue pie.

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Correlative conjunctions, or conjunctions used in pairs, should join sentence parts, sometimes called “matching parts.”

Common correlative conjunctions include

- Either … or
- Neither … no
- Both … and
- If … then
Since *"not only"* is used in the sentence, we should use its partner, **"but also"**.

items.question_content:
**Choose the best conjunction for this sentence.**

If I let you go to Mars, \_\_\_\_\_\_ I’d have to let everyone else go.

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Correlative conjunctions, or conjunctions used in pairs, should join sentence parts, sometimes called “matching parts.”

Common correlative conjunctions include

- Either … or
- Neither … no
- Both … and
- If … then
Since *"if"* is used in the sentence, we should use its partner, **"then"**.

items.question_content:
**Choose the best conjunction for this sentence.**

Whether hiking, fishing, \_\_\_\_\_\_ swimming, Zulaiha loves the outdoors.

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Correlative conjunctions, or conjunctions used in pairs, should join sentence parts, sometimes called “matching parts.”

Common correlative conjunctions include

- Either … or
- Neither … no
- Both … and
- If … then
Since *"Whether"* is used in the sentence, we should use its partner, **"or"**.

items.question_content:
Identify what the **bolded words** are doing in this sentence.

> _You can **either** pick a goldfish **or** a guinea pig to take home._

What are **either** and **or** doing in this sentence?


[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Correlative conjunctions, or conjunctions used in pairs, should join sentence parts, sometimes called “matching parts.”

Common correlative conjunctions include

- Either … or
- Neither … nor
- Both … and
- If … then

Correlative conjunction pairs will always reflect a particular relationship amongst ideas in the sentence. For instance, "either... or" shows that we have to choose between two things.
"Either... or" **presents a choice between options**.

choices.content: Showing a connection between two things
Setting up a rejection of two options

items.question_content:
Identify what the **bolded words** are doing in this sentence.

>_**Neither** Yolanda **nor** Rosianna likes anchovies._

What are **neither** and **nor** doing in this sentence?

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Correlative conjunctions, or conjunctions used in pairs, should join sentence parts, sometimes called “matching parts.”

Common correlative conjunctions include

- Either … or
- Neither … nor
- Both … and
- If … then

Correlative conjunction pairs will always reflect a particular relationship amongst ideas in the sentence. For instance, "either... or" shows that we have to choose between two things.
"Neither... nor" **sets up the rejection of two options**.

choices.content: Setting up a rejection of two options

items.question_content:
Identify what the **bolded words** are doing in this sentence.

>_ **Whether** lounging in a patch of sun **or** hunting down a mouse, my cat pretty much does whatever he wants._

What are **whether** and **or** doing in this sentence?

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Correlative conjunctions, or conjunctions used in pairs, should join sentence parts, sometimes called “matching parts.”

Common correlative conjunctions include

- Either … or
- Neither … nor
- Both … and
- If … then

Correlative conjunction pairs will always reflect a particular relationship amongst ideas in the sentence. For instance, "either... or" shows that we have to choose between two things.
"Whether... or" **sets up a possibility between options**.

choices.content: Setting up a possibility between options
Showing a connection between two things

items.question_content:
Identify what the **bolded words** are doing in this sentence.

 > _Farmer Kate sells **both** nectarines **and** peaches at her booth._

What are **both** and **and** doing in this sentence?

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Correlative conjunctions, or conjunctions used in pairs, should join sentence parts, sometimes called “matching parts.”

Common correlative conjunctions include

- Either … or
- Neither … nor
- Both … and
- If … then

Correlative conjunction pairs will always reflect a particular relationship amongst ideas in the sentence. For instance, "either... or" shows that we have to choose between two things.
"Both... and" **shows a connection between two things**.

choices.content: Showing a connection between two things
Setting up a rejection of two options

items.question_content:
Identify what the **bolded words** are doing in this sentence.

>_**If** you go to the zoo next week, **then** you’ll get to see the newborn pandas._

What are **if** and **then** doing in this sentence?

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Correlative conjunctions, or conjunctions used in pairs, should join sentence parts, sometimes called “matching parts.”

Common correlative conjunctions include

- Either … or
- Neither … nor
- Both … and
- If … then

Correlative conjunction pairs will always reflect a particular relationship amongst ideas in the sentence. For instance, "either... or" shows that we have to choose between two things.
"If... then" **shows the consequences of something**.

choices.content: Setting up a rejection of two options

items.question_content:
Identify what the **bolded words** are doing in this sentence.

>_**Although** Marla hated traveling, she **nevertheless** decided to go to Florida to visit her best friend._

What are **although** and **nevertheless** doing in this sentence?

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Correlative conjunctions, or conjunctions used in pairs, should join sentence parts, sometimes called “matching parts.”

Common correlative conjunctions include

- Either … or
- Neither … nor
- Both … and
- If … then

Correlative conjunction pairs will always reflect a particular relationship amongst ideas in the sentence. For instance, "either... or" shows that we have to choose between two things.
"Although... nevertheless" **shows something contrary to what's expected**.

choices.content: Showing something that’s contrary to what is expected
Setting up a rejection of two options
Presenting a choice between two options

items.question_content:
Identify what the **bolded words** are doing in this sentence.

>_ **Where** there’s the sound of the ice cream truck music, you can bet **there** is a crowd of kids following it._

What are **where** and **there** doing in this sentence?

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Correlative conjunctions, or conjunctions used in pairs, should join sentence parts, sometimes called “matching parts.”

Common correlative conjunctions include

- Either … or
- Neither … nor
- Both … and
- If … then

Correlative conjunction pairs will always reflect a particular relationship amongst ideas in the sentence. For instance, "either... or" shows that we have to choose between two things.
"Where... there" **sets up a cause and effect relationship**.

choices.content: Setting up a cause and effect relationship
Showing something that’s contrary to what is expected
Setting up a rejection of two options

items.question_content:
Identify what the **bolded words** are doing in this sentence.

>_For dinner, my aunt brought **not only** apple pie, **but also** homemade marmalade and fresh bread._

What are **not only** and **but also** doing in this sentence?

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Correlative conjunctions, or conjunctions used in pairs, should join sentence parts, sometimes called “matching parts.”

Common correlative conjunctions include

- Either … or
- Neither … nor
- Both … and
- If … then

Correlative conjunction pairs will always reflect a particular relationship amongst ideas in the sentence. For instance, "either... or" shows that we have to choose between two things.
"Not only... but also" **sets up the delivery of additional information**.

choices.content: Setting up the delivery of additional information
Showing something that’s contrary to what’s expected
